title: Fusion Project Milestones and Commercialization Timeline
executive_question: When will fusion achieve grid-scale commercialization?
required_metrics: Milestone (first plasma, net energy, commercial operation)
comparison_set: ITER, SPARC (CFS), STEP (UK), DEMO (EU), KSTAR, EAST
preferred_sources: UK Atomic Energy Authority STEP updates
data_quality_rule: Use official project updates; note any schedule changes and sources of delay.
narrative_role: Anchor the report's central claim that fusion is not imminent by showing concrete milestones and delays.
pre_exhibit_context: Many stakeholders assume fusion is 10 years away; this exhibit tests that assumption with actual project schedules.
post_exhibit_takeaway: Even the most advanced projects target net-energy demonstration in the 2030s, with commercial plants unlikely before 2040.

title: Levelized Cost of Electricity (LCOE) Comparison by Technology
executive_question: How does fusion LCOE compare to other energy sources?
required_metrics: Technology (fusion, fission, solar PV, onshore wind, gas combined cycle)
comparison_set: Fusion (first-of-a-kind), fission (new build), solar PV, onshore wind, gas CCGT
preferred_sources: Lazard Levelized Cost of Energy Analysis
IEA World Energy Outlook LCOE projections
data_quality_rule: Use published ranges; if fusion LCOE is not available from independent sources, note as developer estimate.
narrative_role: Demonstrate that fusion is currently uncompetitive and likely to remain so for decades.
pre_exhibit_context: Fusion advocates claim cost parity; this exhibit provides independent LCOE estimates.
post_exhibit_takeaway: First-of-a-kind fusion LCOE is projected at $100-200/MWh, well above solar ($20-40) and wind ($30-60) by 2035.

title: Cumulative Investment in Fusion Energy (Public and Private)
executive_question: Who is funding fusion and how much?
required_metrics: Type (public government, private equity, venture capital)
Entity (US DOE, EU, China, private companies)
comparison_set: Public vs private investment by year
preferred_sources: Fusion Industry Association annual investment reports
data_quality_rule: Use official budget documents and verified funding rounds; note any estimates.
narrative_role: Show the scale of capital flowing into fusion and the gap to commercial viability.
pre_exhibit_context: Fusion has attracted billions but needs much more; this exhibit quantifies the investment trajectory.
post_exhibit_takeaway: Private fusion investment reached $6 billion by 2024, but $50 billion+ may be needed for commercial plants.

title: Fusion Energy Adoption Scenarios to 2050
executive_question: What is the potential market size for fusion under different scenarios?
comparison_set: Three scenarios (optimistic, moderate, pessimistic) over time
data_quality_rule: If only two endpoints are available, use CAGR from a credible forecast and mark intermediate years as estimates.
narrative_role: Illustrate that fusion will remain a niche source for decades even under optimistic assumptions.
pre_exhibit_context: Market forecasts vary widely; this exhibit shows a range of credible adoption curves.
post_exhibit_takeaway: Under all scenarios, fusion provides less than 1% of global electricity by 2035 and at most 5% by 2050.

title: Government Fusion R&D Funding by Country (Annual)
executive_question: How is government funding for fusion evolving?
required_metrics: Funding amount ($ million, real or nominal)
comparison_set: US, EU, China, Japan, UK over time
preferred_sources: China Ministry of Science and Technology reports
Japan Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology
search_queries: US DOE fusion energy budget 2025 request
data_quality_rule: Use official budget figures; adjust for inflation if comparing real growth; note currency conversions.
narrative_role: Support the hypothesis that policy support is increasing but still insufficient to accelerate timelines dramatically.
pre_exhibit_context: Government funding is a key driver; this exhibit shows trends in major economies.
post_exhibit_takeaway: US and EU fusion budgets have grown ~20% annually since 2020, but remain below $1 billion each per year.

title: Tritium Supply vs. Demand for Fusion Plants
executive_question: Is tritium availability a bottleneck for fusion deployment?
comparison_set: Tritium supply sources vs. demand from planned fusion plants
preferred_sources: IAEA Tritium Supply and Demand reports
search_queries: tritium consumption per fusion reactor kg per year
data_quality_rule: Use official IAEA data; note that tritium breeding is not yet demonstrated at scale.
narrative_role: Highlight a hidden constraint that could limit the number of fusion plants.
pre_exhibit_context: Tritium is a rare radioactive isotope needed for fusion fuel; its supply is limited.
post_exhibit_takeaway: Current tritium production (~0.5 kg/year) supports only a few demonstration plants; breeding technology is unproven.

title: Regulatory and Licensing Progress for Fusion Plants
executive_question: Which countries are best positioned to host first fusion plants?
required_metrics: Regulatory milestone (draft framework, public consultation, final rule, license application)
preferred_sources: US Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) fusion rulemaking
UK Department for Business, Energy & Industrial Strategy fusion regulation
data_quality_rule: Use official government publications; note if regulations are still in draft form.
narrative_role: Show that regulatory frameworks are still being developed, creating uncertainty.
pre_exhibit_context: Fusion lacks a clear licensing pathway; this exhibit tracks regulatory milestones.
post_exhibit_takeaway: Only the UK and US have proposed fusion-specific regulations; other countries rely on fission frameworks.
